strategic_profile.executive_summary: The University of Michigan-Ann Arbor is the flagship public research university of Michigan and one of the most prestigious public institutions in the United States, widely cited as a "Public Ivy" and named to Forbes' 2025 "New Ivies." It pairs elite, top-ranked programs in engineering, business (Ross), medicine and the social sciences with a research enterprise that surpassed $2 billion in annual expenditures for the first time in FY2024 and reached a record ~$2.16B in FY2025. It is a large, comprehensive university combining academic breadth, a dominant Big Ten athletics culture, and a celebrated college-town setting.
strategic_profile.market_position: Positioned at the very top of American public higher education and broadly regarded as peer to private elite universities. Secondary reporting places it in the top 20 nationally and No. 3 among public universities in the US News fall 2025 (2026 edition) rankings, and Forbes named it a 2025 "New Ivy." Its signature draws are the College of Engineering and the Ross School of Business, both consistently ranked among the best in their fields, supported by a $2B+ research enterprise. For applicants it functions as a target/reach school (reported acceptance rate ~18%) that delivers near-Ivy prestige and employer recognition at a public-university scale.
strategic_profile.admissions_philosophy: Michigan uses a holistic, context-based review. Per its Office of Undergraduate Admissions, the process "is designed to consider all aspects of an applicant's record and experience and is not intended to admit applicants solely on the basis of grade point averages, test scores, or any other single criterion." Reviewers weigh academics and course rigor, essays, recommendations, extracurricular involvement, work experience, and impact outside the classroom, evaluated within the context of each student's high school and opportunities. U-M's own research argues this holistic approach boosts both student success and diversity. Notably, legacy status is not considered in the undergraduate holistic review.

analyst_takeaways.insight: Signature programs include the College of Engineering (reported No. 6 undergraduate nationally) and the Ross School of Business (undergraduate reported No. 3 nationally; MBA No. 13 in US News Best Business Schools), with multiple Ross specialties ranked top-five.
analyst_takeaways.implication: Applicants targeting engineering, business, or pre-professional tracks should expect a more competitive, program-specific bar than the university-wide acceptance rate suggests, and should foreground demonstrated fit and rigor in those areas.

analyst_takeaways.insight: U-M surpassed $2 billion in annual research expenditures for the first time in FY2024 (~$2.04B) and reached a record ~$2.16B in FY2025, spanning fields from sustainability to bionics and AI, with 615 inventions and 28 new startups reported.
analyst_takeaways.implication: Strong undergraduate research and lab-access opportunities make it especially attractive to research-oriented and STEM-bound students; applicants can credibly cite research engagement as a fit signal.

analyst_takeaways.insight: Widely classified as a "Public Ivy" and named a Forbes 2025 "New Ivy," with secondary reporting placing it No. 3 among public universities nationally.
analyst_takeaways.implication: Offers near-Ivy brand value and employer recognition at public-university scale and cost (especially in-state), making it a high-value target for students seeking elite outcomes without a small private setting.

analyst_takeaways.insight: Holistic, context-based review evaluates rigor, essays, recommendations, and out-of-classroom impact within the student's high school context; legacy is not considered.
analyst_takeaways.implication: Strong essays and demonstrated impact within a student's specific context can meaningfully move an application; applicants from less-resourced schools are explicitly evaluated against their available opportunities.

analyst_takeaways.insight: Reported overall acceptance rate around 18%, with one of the higher average admitted-student academic profiles among public flagships.
analyst_takeaways.implication: Most applicants should treat U-M as a reach-to-target; out-of-state and international applicants in particular face a tighter bar than the headline rate implies.

campus_dynamics.social_environment: Large, spirited campus culture blending historic academic buildings, busy student neighborhoods, prominent (and competitive) Greek life, and hundreds of student organizations. Big Ten athletics — especially football at Michigan Stadium ("The Big House," 100,000+ capacity) — is a unifying centerpiece of social life. Set in Ann Arbor, ranked No. 2 on WalletHub's 2025 best college towns list, with hubs like the Diag, South University Avenue, and Main Street.
campus_dynamics.transportation_impact: Campus spans ~3,200 acres split between a walkable Central Campus near downtown and a more spacious, residential North Campus (engineering and arts) several miles away. The university runs the free Blue Bus system linking Central Campus, North Campus, residence halls, and Michigan Medicine, and students ride Ann Arbor's TheRide transit free with their MCard. The Central/North split means engineering and arts students rely on shuttles to move between hubs, a notable daily logistics factor.
campus_dynamics.research_impact: A leading public research university with record research volume (~$2.04B FY2024, first time over $2B; ~$2.16B FY2025), funded by federal agencies, foundations, corporations and substantial internal investment (~$663M internal, ~$1.17B federal in FY2024). This scale drives extensive undergraduate research access, invention output, and startup formation, reinforcing its reputation as a research powerhouse.

current_status.test_policy_details: Test-optional: SAT/ACT recommended but not required; considered if submitted. Latest SAT/ACT receipt date 2/1. Not used for academic advising. Institutional exam used for placement.

academic_structure.structure_notes: U-M is organized as multiple independent undergraduate schools and colleges. As of the fall 2025 first-year application cycle (continuing for Fall 2026 / 2026-2027), there are 10 first-year admitting schools/colleges — applicants pick their home school/college on the Common Application. Several other units (School of Public Health, School of Information, Ford School of Public Policy) admit only after a student has enrolled and reached sophomore/junior standing (upper-level/cross-campus apply-in units). Admission to a school/college is largely a per-school decision; some individual majors (notably Computer Science) carry a separate capacity-limited selection on top of school admission.
academic_structure.as_of_cycle: Fall 2026 application cycle (first-year admitting structure unchanged since the fall 2025 expansion to 10 first-year admitting units)

colleges.name: College of Literature, Science, and the Arts (LSA)
colleges.strategic_fit_advice: LSA is U-M's largest college and the default liberal-arts home; it offers 'more than 85 majors, sub-majors, and other degree programs' and you do not declare a major at the point of first-year admission. Best fit for students who want breadth, flexibility, or an undeclared start. Note that Computer Science is a capacity-limited selection even within LSA, so applicants set on CS should request advance selection on the Common App.
colleges.housing_profile: Not stated in the official catalog pages consulted; U-M housing is assigned university-wide rather than by college, so no LSA-specific housing profile is officially documented.
colleges.student_archetype: Liberal-arts-oriented student wanting a flexible, multidisciplinary path across humanities, social sciences, and natural sciences with the option to start undeclared.

colleges.name: College of Engineering
colleges.strategic_fit_advice: Direct first-year admit to the College of Engineering; you enter as an engineering student and declare a specific discipline later. Computer Science within Engineering is a capacity-limited selection, so CS-set applicants should request advance selection on the Common App. The college offers 18 undergraduate majors, all leading to a BS.
colleges.housing_profile: Not officially documented as a college-specific housing profile in the catalog pages consulted (U-M assigns housing university-wide).
colleges.student_archetype: Quantitatively strong, hands-on builder committed to an engineering or computing discipline from the outset.

colleges.name: Stephen M. Ross School of Business
colleges.strategic_fit_advice: As of fall 2025, Ross is a first-year admitting unit — select 'The Stephen M. Ross School of Business' on the Common App. About 80 percent of each BBA class enters as high-school seniors via Preferred Admission. There is a single major (the BBA) with optional specializations. Highly competitive; applicants who want a back-door route can indicate Preferred Admission interest through other schools to transfer in sophomore year.
colleges.housing_profile: Not officially documented as a school-specific housing profile in the catalog pages consulted.
colleges.student_archetype: Business-focused, leadership-oriented applicant certain about an undergraduate business degree from day one.

majors.name: Business Administration (BBA)
majors.admissions_pathway: Direct first-year admission to Ross, or Preferred Admission / sophomore cross-campus transfer (about 80% enter as HS seniors via Preferred Admission). Single major with optional specializations.

colleges.name: College of Pharmacy
colleges.strategic_fit_advice: New first-year admitting unit as of fall 2025. First-year applicants enter the BS in Pharmaceutical Sciences, which is rooted in the basic and pharmaceutical sciences and prepares students for professional health programs, graduate study, and industry; students can gain admission to the PharmD program a year early while completing the bachelor's degree.
colleges.housing_profile: Not officially documented as a college-specific housing profile in the catalog pages consulted.
colleges.student_archetype: Pre-pharmacy / pharmaceutical-sciences-bound student aiming at a research or PharmD pathway.

majors.name: Pharmaceutical Sciences
majors.admissions_pathway: Direct first-year admission to the College of Pharmacy; early-assurance pathway available into the PharmD

colleges.name: A. Alfred Taubman College of Architecture and Urban Planning
colleges.strategic_fit_advice: First-year applicants can select a Taubman program as an additional program option on the Common App. Two undergraduate degrees: BS in Architecture (liberal-arts foundation years 1-2, specialized architectural focus years 3-4) and BS in Urban Technology. A dual-degree path with LSA, Engineering, or Stamps is also available.
colleges.housing_profile: Not officially documented as a college-specific housing profile in the catalog pages consulted.
colleges.student_archetype: Design- and built-environment-focused student interested in architecture or urban systems and technology.

majors.name: Architecture
majors.admissions_pathway: First-year admission to Taubman College

majors.name: Urban Technology
majors.admissions_pathway: First-year admission to Taubman College

colleges.name: Penny W. Stamps School of Art & Design
colleges.strategic_fit_advice: Portfolio-based first-year admitting unit. Students enter a comprehensive Art & Design program (BFA or BA in Art & Design) and customize across studio areas rather than picking a narrow discipline up front; a BFA in Interarts Performance and dual/joint degrees are also offered. Requires a portfolio.
colleges.housing_profile: Not officially documented as a school-specific housing profile in the catalog pages consulted.
colleges.student_archetype: Studio-art / design-driven creative who wants interdisciplinary making across many media.

majors.name: Art & Design
majors.admissions_pathway: Portfolio-based first-year admission to Stamps (BFA or BA option)

majors.name: Art & Design
majors.admissions_pathway: Portfolio-based first-year admission to Stamps (BFA or BA option)

majors.name: Interarts Performance
majors.admissions_pathway: Portfolio-based first-year admission to Stamps

colleges.name: Marsal Family School of Education
colleges.strategic_fit_advice: Now offers first-year admission (in addition to junior transfer). Elementary and Secondary Teacher Education programs lead to a BA or BS in Education with recommendation for State of Michigan teacher certification; the LEAPS program is a four-year education bachelor's preparing students for a broad range of professional careers. Secondary teacher certification can also be added while remaining in another U-M school/college.
colleges.housing_profile: Not officially documented as a school-specific housing profile in the catalog pages consulted.
colleges.student_archetype: Future educator or education-careers student wanting teacher certification or a profession-oriented education degree.

majors.name: Elementary Teacher Education
majors.admissions_pathway: First-year admission or junior transfer; leads to State of Michigan elementary certification

majors.name: Secondary Teacher Education
majors.admissions_pathway: First-year admission or junior transfer; three-term program leading to State of Michigan secondary certification

majors.name: LEAPS (Learning, Equity, and Problem Solving for the Public Good)
majors.admissions_pathway: First-year admission; four-year education bachelor's for a range of professional careers

colleges.name: School of Kinesiology
colleges.strategic_fit_advice: First-year admitting unit offering three undergraduate majors. Movement Science is a strong pre-health pathway (medicine, PT, OT, rehab, biomedical research). Sport Management requires a secondary admission application before the sophomore or junior year. Applied Exercise Science has no secondary application but requires minimum grades in required lower-level coursework to progress to upper-level courses.
colleges.housing_profile: Not officially documented as a school-specific housing profile in the catalog pages consulted.

majors.name: Movement Science
majors.admissions_pathway: First-year admission to the School of Kinesiology

majors.name: Applied Exercise Science
majors.admissions_pathway: First-year admission; no secondary application, but minimum grades in required lower-level coursework are needed to progress to upper-level coursework (specific grade thresholds not stated on the pages consulted)

majors.name: Sport Management
majors.admissions_pathway: First-year admission to Kinesiology, then a required secondary admission application prior to the sophomore or junior year

colleges.name: School of Music, Theatre & Dance
colleges.strategic_fit_advice: First-year admitting unit; most performance programs require an audition and/or pre-screening/portfolio in addition to the academic application. Offers a wide range of degrees: BFA programs (e.g., Acting, Dance, Musical Theatre, Design & Production, Directing, Interarts Performance, Jazz, Performing Arts Technology), BM programs (e.g., Composition, Music Education, Performance), plus BA, BMA, and BTA tracks.
colleges.housing_profile: Not officially documented as a school-specific housing profile in the catalog pages consulted.
colleges.student_archetype: Conservatory-bound performer, composer, or theatre/dance artist who can pass an audition/portfolio review.

colleges.name: School of Nursing
colleges.strategic_fit_advice: First-year admitting unit offering the BSN. Highly competitive; sophomore transfer entry is also possible. Sophomore-transfer prerequisites include English Composition, Introductory Psychology, Developmental Psychology, Organic/Biochemistry, Anatomy and Physiology, and Statistics — but specific first-year direct-admit prerequisite grades/GPAs are not stated on the pages consulted.
colleges.housing_profile: Not officially documented as a school-specific housing profile in the catalog pages consulted.
colleges.student_archetype: Committed pre-nursing student seeking a direct BSN pathway.

majors.name: Nursing
majors.admissions_pathway: Direct first-year admission to the School of Nursing; sophomore-transfer entry also available

colleges.name: School of Public Health
colleges.strategic_fit_advice: Not a first-year admitting unit. Current U-M students apply during sophomore year and, if admitted, complete junior and senior years in the School of Public Health. Applicants must have 54 transferable credits before beginning the program. Plan a first-year home in LSA (or similar) and the SPH prerequisites if this is the goal.
colleges.housing_profile: Not applicable as a first-year housing profile; students enter as juniors. Not officially documented.
colleges.student_archetype: Public-health-bound student who enters via another college first and applies up in sophomore year.

majors.name: Public Health Sciences
majors.admissions_pathway: Apply during sophomore year for junior-year entry; minimum 54 transferable credits required before starting

colleges.name: Gerald R. Ford School of Public Policy
colleges.strategic_fit_advice: Not a first-year admitting unit; an upper-level admitting unit for internal U-M candidates only. Students apply in winter of sophomore year and complete the BA in Public Policy during junior and senior years; junior standing (55 credit hours by the start of the entering fall term) is required. Application includes a resume, U-M transcript, and three short policy essays.
colleges.housing_profile: Not applicable as a first-year housing profile; students enter as juniors. Not officially documented.
colleges.student_archetype: Policy-focused current U-M student applying up in sophomore year.

majors.name: Public Policy
majors.admissions_pathway: Internal U-M candidates only; apply winter of sophomore year for junior-year entry; junior standing (55+ credits) required

colleges.name: School of Information (UMSI)
colleges.strategic_fit_advice: Not a first-year admitting unit. The BSI is entered via cross-campus transfer (current U-M students) or new-transfer admission, typically as a sophomore or junior; a minimum of 54 transferable credit hours and required prerequisite courses must be completed (or in progress) before junior-year entry. Plan a first-year home elsewhere and the BSI prerequisites.
colleges.housing_profile: Not applicable as a first-year housing profile; students enter mid-undergraduate. Not officially documented.
colleges.student_archetype: Information/UX/data-oriented student who transfers in after building prerequisites.

majors.name: Information
majors.admissions_pathway: Cross-campus or new-transfer admission for sophomore/junior entry; minimum 54 transferable credits plus required prerequisites before junior year

major_selection_tactics: Apply directly to the right admitting unit — at Michigan you apply TO a school/college, not to the university generally
Request CS as an advance-selection ('apply-in') major on the Common App — but know that U-M admission is decided independently of CS selection
Use the cross-campus transfer pathway as a deliberate fallback into Engineering (or Ross/CS) — Michigan publishes the exact thresholds
For business, weigh direct first-year Ross admission vs. the sophomore 'Preferred Admission' dual-degree pipeline through another college

financials.tuition_model: Differential by residency. The University of Michigan-Ann Arbor charges separate tuition rates for Michigan residents (in-state) and non-residents (out-of-state); specific tuition/COA dollar figures are intentionally not restated here (they belong to the cost/COA anchor). Mandatory university fees are assessed each semester on top of tuition. (Source framing per finaid.umich.edu type-of-aid pages and Go Blue Guarantee, which describe 'tuition and mandatory university fees' as separate components.)
financials.aid_philosophy: Need-based, demonstrated-need centered, with a residency-based affordability guarantee. U-M's signature commitment is the Go Blue Guarantee: per the finaid.umich.edu Need-based Grants page (archived 2024-2025 cycle), 'U-M will pay full undergraduate tuition and mandatory university fees for up to four years for students eligible for in-state tuition that have family incomes of $75,000 or less and assets below $75,000 and who are pursuing their first bachelor's degree.' This income/asset threshold has since been RAISED: as of the 2026 program year U-M reports the Go Blue Guarantee covers Michigan-resident Ann Arbor undergraduates with family income AND assets up to $125,000 (per University of Michigan news/record and Bridge Michigan, 2026). The Go Blue Guarantee grant 'represents all or a portion of the university's Go Blue Guarantee commitment, which is comprised of a variety of federal and institutional grant aid' and is 'comprised of a variety of funding (Federal Pell Grant, Federal Supplemental Opportunity Grant, Michigan Achievement Scholarship, institutional scholarships and grants, and non-UM tuition scholarships and grants)' (finaid.umich.edu). New undergraduates must file BOTH the FAFSA and the CSS Profile to be considered for all need-based aid. Most institutional scholarships are awarded automatically at admission based on need and academic merit — 'The Office of Financial aid considers students for dozens of scholarships each year. Most are awarded automatically to eligible students upon admission (no additional application required)' (finaid.umich.edu OFA Scholarship Listing). Per the most recent published University of Michigan Common Data Set (2023-2024 CDS, Fall 2022 cohort, obp.umich.edu), U-M met on average 92% of the demonstrated need of full-time undergraduates awarded need-based aid (91% for first-year students).

scholarships.name: Go Blue Guarantee (University of Michigan need-based grant)
scholarships.amount: Covers full undergraduate tuition and mandatory university fees for up to four years; 'Grant amount varies based on need and availability of university funds.' Original eligibility: in-state students with family income and assets of $75,000 or less (finaid.umich.edu, archived 2024-2025 cycle). Expanded for 2026: income and assets up to $125,000; in 2026 also began covering full cost of attendance including housing for eligible teacher-prep/nursing students and former-GBG students pursuing a master's (University of Michigan news/record; Bridge Michigan, 2026).
scholarships.deadline: For new undergraduates: FAFSA + CSS Profile due Nov. 15 (Early Decision) or March 1 (Early Action / Regular Decision); Dec. 15 is the suggested (non-binding) filing date for a preliminary package (finaid.umich.edu, 2026-2027 cycle).
scholarships.benefits: Full tuition + mandatory university fees for up to four years for eligible Michigan-resident undergraduates pursuing a first bachelor's degree; renewable but must reapply for aid and meet eligibility each year. Comprised of a mix of federal and institutional grant aid (Pell, FSEOG, Michigan Achievement Scholarship, institutional scholarships/grants, and non-UM tuition scholarships/grants).
scholarships.application_method: Complete the FAFSA and the CSS Profile; no separate Go Blue Guarantee application. Must be eligible for in-state tuition and pursuing a first bachelor's degree.

scholarships.name: Fairfax (Jean) Scholarship
scholarships.type: merit + need (enrollment scholarship, considered at admission)
scholarships.amount: Funding amounts vary based on residency; 'see individual letters for terms and conditions.' Resident students are considered for Fairfax tuition funding.
scholarships.benefits: Four-year enrollment scholarship for resident undergraduates ('Resident students are considered for the Fairfax tuition funding'); 'Awarded to resident and non-resident undergraduates. Funding amounts vary based on residency.'
scholarships.application_method: No application — 'Opportunities for the four-year Fairfax and Tappan scholarships are considered during the admission process. Students do not apply for these.'

scholarships.name: Tappan Scholarship
scholarships.type: merit (enrollment scholarship for non-residents, considered at admission)
scholarships.amount: A set monetary scholarship for non-residents; 'See individual letters for terms and conditions' ('non-residents are considered for a set monetary scholarship for the Tappan scholarship').
scholarships.benefits: Four-year enrollment scholarship for selected non-resident undergraduate students.
scholarships.application_method: No application — considered automatically during the admission process. 'Students do not apply for these.'

scholarships.name: Detroit Promise Scholarship
scholarships.amount: Four years of tuition and mandatory fees at the U-M Ann Arbor campus.
scholarships.benefits: 'It offers four years of tuition and mandatory fees at the UM-Ann Arbor campus.'
scholarships.application_method: Recipients 'are selected through a process handled by the Michigan Education Excellence Foundation and the Detroit Regional Chamber Foundation in partnership with U-M' — not a direct U-M financial-aid application.

scholarships.name: Wolverine Pathways Scholarship
scholarships.amount: Four years of tuition and mandatory fees.
scholarships.benefits: 'Offered to students who complete the Wolverine Pathways program, giving them four years of tuition and mandatory fees. Selected undergraduates are from Detroit, Southfield, and Ypsilanti public school districts.'
scholarships.application_method: Earned by completing the Wolverine Pathways pre-college program (no separate aid application for the scholarship itself).

scholarships.name: Institutional non-need-based (merit) scholarships / grants
scholarships.amount: Average institutional non-need-based scholarship/grant award (excluding athletic awards and tuition benefits) of $6,213 for all full-time undergraduates and $4,425 for first-time freshmen, per the 2023-2024 Common Data Set, Section H2A line O (obp.umich.edu, Fall 2022 cohort).
scholarships.benefits: Awarded to students with no financial need based on academic and other merit criteria; mostly awarded automatically at admission. Per CDS, 3,379 full-time undergraduates (1,030 first-time freshmen) received institutional non-need-based scholarship/grant aid in this cohort.
scholarships.application_method: Most are 'awarded automatically to eligible students upon admission (no additional application required)'; some donors 'request more information and an application' (finaid.umich.edu OFA Scholarship Listing).

credit_policies.philosophy: Credit is granted for college-level work demonstrated through high exam scores, and the granting of credit/placement is determined by the individual schools, colleges, and departments (with LSA guidelines as the default). Per the U-M Office of Undergraduate Admissions: "As a result of having already successfully completed college-level work, some students can be admitted with advanced standing at the university... in addition to completing your AP/IB course(s), you must also perform successfully on the exam and earn a high score. Advanced Standing is only granted to students who have high exam scores." The AP Guidelines page adds: "Please refer to the guidelines for the University of Michigan school or college you will be applying to... Schools and colleges not listed above award credit according to LSA guidelines." The LSA Credit-by-Examination policy notes the faculty has delegated credit decisions to departments: "Recognizing that students may have background in particular academic areas, the faculty has left it to each department to decide if it is possible for students to earn credit by examination."

ap_policy.general_rule: AP credit and course placement are granted per published charts that vary by school/college; LSA guidelines are the default for schools not separately listed. Most exams require a score of 4 or 5, though a few exams/departments grant credit at a 3 (per the published charts). Official text (AP Guidelines page): "The following charts outline the guidelines for the granting of credit and course placement for acceptable achievement on the Advanced Placement program examinations. Please refer to the guidelines for the University of Michigan school or college you will be applying to... Schools and colleges not listed above award credit according to LSA guidelines." (Charts show common thresholds of "4 or 5".)
exceptions: Ross School of Business (BBA): "For Ross School of Business first-year students – hours awarded according to LSA Guidelines. The Ross School of Business accepts a maximum of 30 credit hours completed prior to the first year (inclusive of test and transfer credit) towards the BBA degree."

College of Engineering has its own AP chart and specific exclusions, e.g. "The College of Engineering does not award credit for the AB subscore of the BC exam" and "The College of Engineering does not award credit for the AP Physics B, AP Physics 1, or AP Physics 2 exams."
ap_policy.usage: Per the LSA Test Credit policy: "AP credit may be used as general elective credit, to fulfill the language requirement, and to fulfill major/minor prerequisites. AP may not be used to fulfill distribution requirements." Also: "Test credit is considered out-of residence credit." (Note: per the AP/IB credit FAQ ecosystem, AP also cannot satisfy the First-Year Writing Requirement — this specific limitation was not re-verified on a fetched official page in this pass, so it is not asserted here.)

ib_policy.general_rule: U-M awards credit only for IB Higher Level (HL) examinations; Standard Level exams earn no credit. Official text (IB Guidelines page): "To receive credit for International Baccalaureate (IB) examinations, an official transcript of examination grades must be officially sent by the IB Office directly to the Office of Undergraduate Admissions... The Office of Undergraduate Admissions only awards credit for IB Higher Level examinations." Usage (LSA Test Credit page): "IB credit may be used as general elective credit and to fulfill distribution requirements, the language requirement, and major/minor prerequisites."

tools: Transfer Credit Equivalency (TCE) search tool. Official text: "Students can check to see if a course has been approved for transfer through the Transfer Credit Equivalency(TCE) webpage at https://transfercredit.ugadmiss.umich.edu/." If a course is not listed: "current U-M students may request a pre-evaluation from the Office of Undergraduate Admissions by completing an online form at https://www.ugadmiss.umich.edu/TCE//Student/CT_TCEForm.aspx."

Michigan Transfer Agreement (MTA) pre-evaluation for community-college distribution credit. Official text: "Courses that are part of each Michigan community college's Michigan Transfer Agreement curriculum have been pre-evaluated for distribution credit which is posted at https://admissions.umich.edu/michigan-transfer-agreement."
transfer_articulation.restrictions: Key LSA transfer-credit restrictions (Transfer Credit Evaluation Policy, Effective May 2024): (1) Grade floor — "Only courses with a grade of C or better will be accepted for transfer credit. This includes all transferable credit earned outside the University of Michigan" (UM-Dearborn and UM-Flint courses transfer regardless of grade). (2) Accreditation — credit transfers only if "Course work was completed at an accredited college or university." (3) Credit cap — "Up to 60 credits may be transferred from other colleges and universities to count toward the 120 required for a LSA degree. Students who have completed an associate's degree that required 62 credits may transfer 62 credits." (4) Out-of-residence — transfer credit is out-of-residence credit subject to the Residency Policy; "LSA residency requires that a student earn 30 credits in LSA." (5) No double-counting toward another degree — "Credit cannot be transferred from another school if that credit is also being counted toward another baccalaureate or graduate or professional degree. Special Joint Degree Programs are exceptions to this policy." (6) Grades do not transfer into GPA except UM-Dearborn/UM-Flint. (7) Student responsibility — "It is the responsibility of the student to confirm that a course will transfer before enrolling in the course." (8) Unmatched courses post as Transfer General Credit (TRGENCR 101X) that "will not count toward any specific degree requirement but will be elective credit."

what_it_takes: {"point":"Take the most rigorous course load your high school offers (max AP/IB/honors). Multiple community/advice sources independently call course rigor the single biggest lever — and the Common Data Set lists 'Course rigor' and 'Academic GPA' as the two 'Very Important' factors.","sources":["CollegeVine community FAQ","Quad Education advice guide","CollegeVine blog (citing Common Data Set)"],"multi_source":true}

{"point":"A very high GPA is effectively table stakes — community sources converge on ~3.9 average for the admitted class (with a large share at 4.0). Crowdsourced framing: 'aim for 4.0 to be competitive,' high GPA is necessary but not sufficient.","sources":["CollegeVine blog (cites 3.9 avg, ~39% with 4.0)","Quad Education (cites 3.9 avg)","CollegeVine 'how to get in' search snippet"],"multi_source":true}

{"point":"Depth over breadth in extracurriculars — communities repeatedly say Michigan rewards genuine, sustained commitment and leadership in a few activities over a long padded list. Several frame it as developing a clear 'spike'/Tier 1-2 activity rather than being generically well-rounded.","sources":["CollegeVine community FAQ (multiple FAQ pages)","Niche admissions overview ('well-rounded individual... commitment and leadership')","Quad Education"],"multi_source":true}

{"point":"Out-of-state applicants face a notably tougher bar than in-state — community members repeatedly cite a large in-state vs OOS acceptance-rate gap (forum figures circulated: ~39% in-state vs ~14% OOS for a recent cycle) and advise OOS applicants to be clearly above the typical admitted profile.","sources":["College Confidential Class of 2029/2030 RD threads (in-state ~39% vs OOS ~14% figures cited by users)","College Confidential ('above the norm of who they send to Michigan' — user Knowsstuff)"],"multi_source":true}

{"point":"Essays carry real weight in a holistic read — community consensus is that strong, specific essays (especially a well-researched 'Why this school/major') can be the differentiator among the many high-GPA applicants. CDS lists essays as 'Important.'","sources":["PrepScholar essay guide","CollegeVine community FAQ + blog","CollegeVine blog (CDS: essays 'Important')"],"multi_source":true}
common_activities: {"activity":"Sustained leadership in a school club or organization (founding/leading rather than just being a member)","sources":["CollegeVine community FAQ","Niche reviews"],"multi_source":true}

{"activity":"Community service / volunteering tied to a genuine cause (e.g., literacy or community-impact campaigns)","sources":["CollegeVine essay-examples blog (literacy campaign, volunteer network)","Quad Education ('doing amazing things in their communities')"],"multi_source":true}

{"activity":"Athletics or coaching — varsity/club sports or coaching younger kids (e.g., a junior basketball team)","sources":["CollegeVine essay-examples blog (coaching 'Thunderdragons')","Niche/UMich campus-life aggregate (intramural & club sports prominent)"],"multi_source":true}

{"activity":"Research experience or strong STEM/academic project work (lab work, science fair, independent research)","sources":["CollegeVine essay-examples blog (psychology lab/honors thesis)","Niche academics (research opportunities a recurring theme)"],"multi_source":true}

{"activity":"Debate / Model UN / speech and other competitive academic activities","sources":["CollegeVine essay-examples blog (parliamentary debate)","UMich admissions blog / Empowerly-style guides (debate cited among valued involvements)"],"multi_source":true}

{"activity":"Visual or performing arts (drawing, music, theater) with peer-teaching or collaboration","sources":["CollegeVine essay-examples blog (drawing classes, peer feedback)","Niche campus-life (performing/visual arts organizations widely cited)"],"multi_source":true}

{"activity":"Journalism / writing — school newspaper, publications, op-eds","sources":["CollegeVine essay-examples blog (school newspaper, published article)","CollegeVine community FAQ (writing/intellectual engagement valued)"],"multi_source":true}

{"activity":"Paid work or family responsibilities — community sources stress Michigan explicitly counts a weekend job or caregiving as legitimate involvement, not just resume-style activities","sources":["Quad Education (quotes UMich: 'hold a weekend job to support your family, every type of involvement counts')","CollegeVine community FAQ (impact/commitment over prestige)"],"multi_source":true}
essay_tips: {"tip":"Be extremely specific on the 'Why Michigan / why this major' essay — name actual programs, courses, professors, labs, and facilities; generic praise ('great academics') is the most-cited way to underperform.","sources":["PrepScholar essay guide","CollegeVine community FAQ + blog","Quad Education","CollegeVine essay-examples blog"],"multi_source":true}

{"tip":"On the Community essay, ground it in one specific community and a concrete anecdote/role — show your place within it and how it shaped you; your topic doesn't need to be exotic, it needs self-awareness.","sources":["PrepScholar essay guide","CollegeVine essay-examples blog","Accepted.com / CollegeEssayAdvisors-style guides (per search snippets)"],"multi_source":true}

{"tip":"Show, don't tell — use vivid details, dialogue, and imagery in an authentic personal voice rather than abstract statements; let your voice come through.","sources":["PrepScholar essay guide","CollegeVine essay-examples blog","CollegeVine community FAQ"],"multi_source":true}

{"tip":"Answer every part of each prompt, keep the two supplements distinct from each other and from your Common App essay, and use the word count fully; proofread for any errors.","sources":["PrepScholar essay guide (avoid overlap, no typos)","CollegeVine community FAQ ('addressing all parts of each prompt')"],"multi_source":true}
red_flags: {"flag":"Generic, low-effort essays — especially a 'Why Michigan' that could be copy-pasted to any school. Communities repeatedly say vague enthusiasm without specifics is a top reason strong-stat applicants get deferred/rejected.","sources":["PrepScholar essay guide","Quad Education (generic 'UMich has great academics' underperforms)","CollegeVine community FAQ"],"multi_source":true}

{"flag":"Padded, shallow activity lists — many activities with little depth, leadership, or measurable impact. Community consensus: this reads as resume-stuffing and hurts you versus a focused profile.","sources":["CollegeVine community FAQ (multiple pages)","Quad Education (lack of reflection on impact weakens applications)"],"multi_source":true}

{"flag":"Under-rigorous schedule relative to what your school offers — taking easier classes to protect GPA is widely cited as a mistake since course rigor is weighted above almost everything else; this especially sinks OOS applicants who need to clearly exceed the norm.","sources":["College Confidential RD threads (deferrals attributed to weaker rigor/ECs/leadership)","CollegeVine blog (CDS rigor 'Very Important')","Quad Education"],"multi_source":true}

insights.insight: Forum users repeatedly note a steep in-state vs out-of-state gap (figures circulated: ~39% in-state vs ~14% OOS for a recent cycle) and advise OOS applicants they must clearly exceed the typical admitted profile. OPINION — user-cited, not official.

'If your student is above the norm of who they send to Michigan or at that level then that is good' — paraphrased community heuristic that being merely at the average isn't enough, especially OOS. OPINION.
Community/expert answers stress Michigan 'is more interested in commitment and leadership than breadth of involvement' and favors a clear 'spike' / Tier 1-2 activities over a long thin list. OPINION.

Students describe a 'work hard, play hard' campus with 1,600+ orgs; commonly cited involvements include Greek life (~20% participation), intramural/club sports, cultural/academic orgs, and research. OPINION/aggregate.

Be 'extremely specific' on Why Michigan — name classes, programs, professors, facilities — and tell the Community essay through a concrete anecdote in your own voice; avoid clichés and overlap between essays. OPINION.

Admitted-student examples succeed by weaving program research into a personal trajectory and grounding the community essay in a specific role (coaching a kids' team, leading a literacy campaign, lab research). The common failure mode cited is focusing on what UMich offers instead of who you are. OPINION.

insights.source: Quad Education + CollegeVine blog (citing Common Data Set)
insights.insight: Generic essays and shallow/padded activity lists are repeatedly named as why high-GPA applicants get passed over; under-rigorous schedules are penalized because the CDS lists Course Rigor and GPA as the only two 'Very Important' factors (this CDS weighting is verifiable fact, not opinion). MIXED — opinion on the failure modes, fact on the CDS weighting.
